content:
The people who remain can explain the scars.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The all-hands slide said we were “retaining top talent.”
Forty-one people left in March. Twelve stayed on the platform team. The ones who remained were not the highest leetcode scorers from the last hiring cycle. They were the ones who could reboot the job runner without waking three other teams, explain why invoice batching still writes to a table named tmp_fix_2019, and tell you which on-call runbook page is lying.
I know because I was one of the twelve.
Nobody gave us a medal. They gave us the backlog, the pager, and a headcount plan that assumed we could absorb forty-one people’s worth of context with twelve tired brains and the same sprint velocity.
The Myth of the Best Coder
Companies talk about layoffs like talent is a sorted list. Cut the bottom third. Keep the stars.
Production does not work that way.
The “best coder” on paper — clean abstractions, fast feature shipping, strong system design interview — often joined after the ugly decisions were already baked in. They inherit greenfield services with modern frameworks and clear ownership boundaries.
The survivor who looks average on a promotion rubric might be the only person who knows:
→ Which cron job silently retries failed webhooks with exponential backoff capped wrong → Why the EU shard still runs Postgres 12 and who signed the exception → That the “temporary” feature flag from 2021 controls whether enterprise SSO bypasses MFA → How to unpause the queue without double-processing messages → Which vendor contract expires in September and triggers a manual certificate swap
That knowledge is not on GitHub. It is not in Confluence because Confluence was last updated by someone who no longer has a badge.
Layoff survivors are not automatically the best coders. They are the people who own the scars.
What Ownership Looks Like After Headcount Math
April was not heroic. April was arithmetic.
Same number of services. Fewer people. More tickets labeled P2 that used to be P1 because there was nobody left to assign P1 to.
I spent a Tuesday not writing features. I spent it tracing why refund webhooks duplicated after the payments team lost their senior SRE. The webhook idempotency key lived in a Redis namespace documented nowhere. I found it because I had been paged for the same bug in 2022 and left myself a Slack message to future-me that I actually searched for.
My teammate Priya spent Wednesday explaining to a new director why we cannot “just migrate to Kubernetes” this quarter. Not because Kubernetes is bad. Because three batch jobs assume host-level paths, one legacy service binds to a static IP whitelisted by a bank, and the migration budget line item left with the people who wrote the original estimate.
The director heard “resistance.” Priya was doing time math. Migration is not a technology choice when contracts and file paths are involved. Migration is a balance sheet item spread across quarters nobody allocated.
The Hidden Cost Nobody Puts in the Spreadsheet
Survivors pay a tax that does not show up in compensation bands:
→ Context switching between systems they did not build but now must operate → Explaining the same legacy constraint in every new planning meeting → Delayed career growth because roadmap work waits while fires get contained → Emotional load from empty desks and Slack channels that still ping → Interview pressure from recruiters who assume survivors are “available” because the company stumbled
I am not asking for sympathy. I am naming the economics.
When headcount drops forty percent and service count drops zero percent, the remaining engineers become a walking bus factor with legs that hurt. The company saves salary. The survivors spend time that was supposed to go toward paying down debt on keeping the lights on.
That is a trade. Finance understands it when you say it plainly. Engineering orgs often pretend the trade does not exist because pretending keeps the roadmap green.
What Good Leadership Does With Survivors
The teams that recovered fastest did not treat survivors like winners of a hunger games. They did three boring things:
→ Named explicit ownership per ugly system, with relief valves — contractors, vendor support, reduced scope — instead of heroic assumptions → Funded documentation sprints as real work, not volunteer nights, because the people who know the scars are also the people carrying the pager → Stopped comparing survivor output to pre-layoff team output without adjusting WIP limits → Hired for operational temperament, not only feature velocity, when backfilling
Bad leadership treats survivors as proof the layoff worked. “See, the team is still shipping.” Shipping is not the same as healthy. A team can ship while silently losing the only person who understands settlement reconciliation.
Good leadership asks survivors: what will break in ninety days if nothing changes? Then puts that answer on a slide with a budget line.
If You Are a Survivor
Practical notes from the trenches, not inspiration poster material:
→ Write down what only you know, even if it is ugly and incomplete. Future you will thank present you at 2 A.M. → Refuse silent scope expansion. If headcount halved, WIP must halve too. Say that in writing. → Negotiate with time, not guilt. Your leverage is operational knowledge. Use it ethically — not as hostage-taking, as clarity about risk. → Do not confuse company loyalty with unlimited availability. Burnout does not fix tmp_fix_2019. → Update your resume. Not because you should leave immediately. Because survivors who know the scars are employable everywhere legacy systems exist. That is most places.
If leadership asks you to “do more with less” without naming what stops, they are asking you to fund the gap with your nights. That is a loan they will not repay.
What Hiring Gets Wrong After a Layoff
Backfill job posts often ask for “5+ years Kubernetes” and “strong system design” because those bullets scan well on LinkedIn.
What the team actually needs in month two:
→ Someone willing to read shell scripts named fix2.sh without rewriting them on day one → Patience for systems where the test suite is a prayer → Ability to document while firefighting without treating documentation as lesser work → Comfort saying "I do not know yet" in front of directors who want certainty
We interviewed six candidates who gave beautiful answers about microservices decomposition. None of them asked why invoice batching still touched tmp_fix_2019. The seventh asked. We hired the seventh.
Not because Kubernetes answers were wrong. Because production ownership starts with curiosity about scars, not disgust at them.
Recruiters love survivors as candidates too — “you clearly can handle pressure.” Translation: you kept the pager while the org shrank. Negotiate accordingly. Calm is a skill with market value.
Time Debt Compounds Faster Than Tech Debt
Tech debt is code that should be cleaner. Time debt is work that required hours nobody had because hours went to survival.
After our layoff, time debt looked like:
→ Migration docs half-written because the author left mid-paragraph → Runbooks referencing Slack channels archived in the reduction → Quarterly dependency upgrades skipped twice → Pairing sessions cancelled so one person could cover three on-call rotations
Time debt does not show in SonarQube. It shows when a new hire spends three weeks reconstructing a deployment pipeline the old team knew by muscle memory.
Survivors pay time debt with their calendars. Leadership can pay it with budget — contractors, reduced scope, explicit maintenance sprints — or pretend the debt does not exist until an auditor or customer notices.
Pretending is cheaper on quarterly reports. Expensive everywhere else.
The Scar Tissue Is the Asset
Six months later, our platform team was smaller and slower on greenfield features. We were also faster on incidents that touched legacy paths because the scars were concentrated in twelve heads instead of diffused across forty-one.
That is not an argument for layoffs. Layoffs are destructive. It is an argument for honesty about what remains when numbers shrink.
The best coders in the interview loop might rebuild your checkout flow beautifully. The survivors might be the only ones who can keep the old checkout flow from double-charging Munich while that rebuild ships.
Companies that confuse those roles learn expensively.
If you are evaluating a team after restructuring, do not ask who writes the cleanest code. Ask who can explain the ugliest system without opening Jira. That person is not overhead. That person is your production insurance policy.
For the system design, operational ownership, and backend economics that turn scar tissue into survivable architecture — The Production Engineering Library: The Complete Collection for Production Systems, System Design & Backend Engineering is what I recommend when survivors ask what to study between incidents.
Rehearse the judgment calls legacy systems demand — rollback paths, idempotency, batch job failure modes — on Froquiz: 10,000+ scenario questions across SQL, Docker, Git, AWS, Java, Python, JavaScript, React, microservices, plus Senior Dev Challenge problems shaped like on-call, not homework.
→ Froquiz
